sentences.text: --home, I couldn't sleep a whole night.
It just made me nervous.
The next day, when I came to work, the [?
Hollande ?] asked me, what is the matter with you?
How come you're so distracted?
I said, I saw something very terrible.
They killed the guy.
Oh, they said.
Ha!
Don't worry about, ah-- they-- just behave.
Just do what you have to do and be careful.
Because these people, they don't care.
They can kill any day.
If they could kill us all, they would kill us all.
But they need us.
So just do your job.
I said, fine, OK.
A few days later, after supper, a couple SS men came to our barracks.
Actually, our barracks were houses.
We had three floors, and rooms.
And so we were in the floors.
But they came, and they had a loudspeaker.
And they yelled, everybody rouse!
Hm.
So I figured, oh boy, what is that now?
And when we were outside, he said, we will show you Jews.
And so, we had to be in row four and marched.
And we marched outside, behind the barracks-- behind the buildings.
And I thought, they going to throw us in the river-- in the ocean.
Because it was right by the ocean, where they-- but just before, they-- they stopped.
And they said-- [GERMAN].
Take off all the clothes.
Oh, boy.
So I started.
You know, everybody started to shake.
And, but, what could we do?
Because they went around with what you beat horses--
--you know, a horse whip.
And who wasn't-- we're taking that they whipped.
You know, so we took off to clothes.
And we stood there.
And then he took out the newspaper.
And it was And in he read an article that a Jew in New York was writing an article that to have peace in this world, you have to castrate all Germans.
And then he said, we will-- we will show you castration of all Germans.
And they started-- we were all in the nude-- they started to-- to make us go exercises.
And we had to jump up and down.
And we have to lay and [? strumple-- ?] jump with the feet.
And they came in with the whip, and whipped everybody who was not doing it.
You can't do better than that?
And they whipped, you know.
And, well, I only got whipped once.
I was lucky.
[LAUGHS] And to-- and it went on for approximately, I would say, over an hour.
Until they were finally tired themselves.
They beat some people to blood, you know.
And then finally, they said, so you can go and wash up.
And you go to bed.
And tomorrow you go back to work.
So we couldn't even march.
We could barely walk to the-- to the place, and we went in the shower.
And some people have to go to the-- to the infirmity-- to-- to have their wounds-- you know, what they were beaten up.
You know, to have to put a plaster on.
Anyhow, I started not to like that place.
And all of a sudden, I [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I felt sick.
And I had jaundice.
I was-- got yellow.
And I went to the doctor.
The doctor was a Jewish doctor.
And the doctor said, well, it's probably your excitement.
And all you have to do-- keep away from all milk products, and just eat starch as much as you can, and very little meat, and you'll be all right.
And-- so I didn't have any milk products.
And I just ate bread and soup.
And about three days or so, it went away.
And I quiet down.
And then all of a sudden, a few days later, there came an order all the Jews have to go back to the camp just out where they came from.
The war with Germany started-- with Russia started.
And they didn't want it to have no Jews, and no Russians, and anybody up-- and Polish, or whatever they had.
And this only the [?
Hollande ?] and the French and the German left there.
Everybody else had to go out.
They prepared-- they probably prepared, at that time-- they prepared to invade England or something like that.
They didn't want no-- so anyhow we went back to Gurs-- not Gurs-- to Rivesaltes.
So I went back to Rivesaltes, and I was visiting my mother.
I couldn't find her.
She was, by that time, moved to a family what took her in to be a nanny to the kids.
I found out later, the Red Cross-- the Swiss Hospital-- arranged it.
And they didn't want to give me the address.
They kept it a secret, just to keep her safe.
My father was in the hospital.
And he's supposed to have an operation done on the eyes.
And that was 1942-- or maybe just before my birthday, I believe.
It may have been January or so.
I was just-- just going to 17.
Just going to 17.
And that man what was the [?
Andlauser, ?] he wasn't there anymore.
And so I didn't know what to do.
And I ran away.
And I-- well, I didn't actually run away by myself.
But there was a guy came to the camp.
He must have been about early '30s.
And he said, well, come with us to the forest.
You know, we work for General Degaulle.
And we try to be with the-- with the French partisan, with the FFI.
And we we're trying to liberate France, and we going to make the Germans a bad time.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
There were just boys.
Well, they were five.
I went with them.
And--
No.
I don't.
I don't even remember the names of my 50 friends what were there with me.
I don't remember any of these names.
The only name I remember is Abbai Glazberg.
Abbai Glazberg, I'd found out when I was there for a while, that he is a Jew.
But he is an Abbai.
Abbai means he is some kind of a priest.
And he is in contact with Degaulle.
But he is-- he has an intermediary-- what-- a go-between, which was a lady.
And she was a refugee from-- from the Communist Party-- from the French Communist Party.
But I don't remember her name, either.
I only remember Abbai-- Abbai Glazberg.
It was, you know-- it took me so-- you know.
That-- Abbai?
Glazberg?
So that's why I remember his name.
Well, I would say that we stayed about-- till about July-- July in 1942.
And we were surrounded by the French militia.
By the French fascist party--
And they surrounded us.
And they supposed to deliver us to Lyon.
In Lyon, there was the Gestapo-- the big Gestapo, you know.
And they wanted, probably, to find out more about our work.
But somehow, on the way to Lyon, we passed through Grenoble.
And when we came to Grenoble, they mixed us up.
They had assembled all the Jews from the whole district to transport them to Drancy.
Drancy was the concentration of all the people to go to Poland and the concentration camps in Paris.
It's just outside of Paris.
So actually, I was lucky again.
Because I didn't end up in Lyon to be interviewed or interrogated by the Gestapo.
And we ended up in Drancy.
And I stayed in Drancy about a week.
And they loaded us up in cattle cars, and they brought us to Auschwitz.
We didn't come in into--
--Auschwitz.
Oh.
Go by train?
Yeah, in the cattle cars.
I think it took us four nights.
Four nights-- four-- three days, four nights.
No.
They didn't open up.
They-- they gave bread.
So many people in the car-- they gave us so many breads.
And they gave us so many buckets of water.
And they gave us so many buckets empty-- to relieve ourselves.
And there were whole families.
There were men, women, and children.
I was alone.
And they never opened up the wagons.
And by the time we got to Auschwitz and they opened up the doors, we had six dead people.
In our car, we had six dead people.
Out of the six dead people, we had one child what was maybe-- maybe-- five years old.
But one.
One little girl.
Anyhow, when the doors opened up, the SS was there.
They said, now, we are sorry that you had such a bad ride, but the bahn-- the train was delayed.
But we're going to-- we're going to compensate for it.
So who wants to work can come down, and we'll go into a work camp.
And who wants to stay with the family, stays inside-- doesn't have to come out.
OK?
And--
Just outside the gate of Auschwitz.
Because when I turned around-- when I jumped out and I turned around, I saw the gate.
I was one of the last ones in these wagons.
I think I was the third last one.
There were two more.
They didn't take out the dead people.
Because, they said, oh, it's not long.
We can do nothing here.
You go after these are done, we-- you move over, and they will look after them.
OK.
So I jumped down.
And we had to be in line of eight.
So I was the first one on the next line.
And there was a young German soldier.
Just an ordinary soldier, in green uniform-- not an SS, and not an SA.
He was just a French soldier.
He must have been maybe 18 years old-- maybe 17.
And young fellow-- he said to me, you know-- in German, he said-- do you understand German?
I said yeah.
I speak good German.
He said, you know what?
You are lucky.
You are going to stay alive.
So I looked at him, and I said, why?
And he said, be quiet.
Don't say nothing.
You are going to stay alive.
You're lucky.
So-- and then we had more rows, you know?
And he moved with the rows.
And I never saw him again.
But that gave me to think.
And I figured, why should he tell me you'll stay alive?
What about the rest?
Don't they go in a family camp?
I didn't know.
I just didn't know.
No.
I didn't know.
Not even in the partisans.
I didn't know.
So they marched us to a camp.
The camp must have been maybe, at the most, five kilometer away.
And they gave us to eat, and the shower.
We went to the shower.
And I cannot remember any woman or girl to jump out.
Not one.
There were only men and boys.
Yeah.
So in that camp I was two days, at the most-- maybe three.
One day, I worked.
They-- they worked autobahn.
That was a fast car road.
I don't know.
He was preparing for-- for some quick transport.
But anyhow, I only worked there one day.
Then, the third day-- oh, yes.
The third day was a Monday.
The one day I worked was Saturday.
And Sunday there was no work.
They let everybody rest.
And then it was a Monday.
And they came with buses.
And they loaded-- well, they called my name.
And because they registered--
No.
I had names at that time.
No numbers.
And they told us to go into the buses.
And we went into the buses.
And we drove for, oh, I would say maybe 10 hours from-- from Auschwitz.
And we came-- well, it must have been early in the afternoon.
And we came to a place, and it was called Konigshutte.
And in Konigshutte, they had at that point a factory of some kind.
But on the-- on the big outside big wall-- they had this big long warehouse.
And that warehouse was empty with anything-- except they had a two-story beds.
One, two-- three-story beds.
Three-story beds.
And bunk beds.
Yeah, we call them bunk beds here.
But three story.
And so they gave us each-- you know-- a bed.
And in the bank-- in the back of these bunk beds, there was benches-- just straight benches.
And so they told us that you put your clothes on the benches, and-- and you have the bed.
And in the front, there were also benches.
You can eat, you know, when you get your food.
Then, right there on that same day, they arranged into groups-- who is going to be working in the back of Konigshutte-- in the factory there-- and who is going to go to Laurahutte.
Now--
The-- the boss-- I mean the commander or the captain of this camp-- was a fireman.
And he was-- like we called him a valse deutche.
That means he was Polish, but he was German descent.
And he actually was like a German.
And the German made him as the boss of that whole outfit.
And he was a sadist.
And he had-- in the back of that building-- he had a little building which they installed the kitchen.
And he had women working there-- Jewish girls-- women.
And he-- he somehow liked to give punishment.
If anybody didn't do something right, or didn't make the bed right, or didn't put this stuff nice on the benches, he took the name.
And in the evening, everybody had to sit on the benches all along.
And in the middle, they put a low table.
And the guy got with a-- with a rubber cable on his back.
And the way he liked to do it is everybody had to come.
Even the women and the girls had to sit, you know, and had to watch it too.
And the guy who get the punishment had to undress completely nude, and lay down.
And he got five with the rubber-- or 10, 15, 20.
And one time, we had two people-- they ran away.
And they caught them.
And it was in wintertime then.
And they beat them every day with 25, and nude.
They had to run outside and go in the snow.
And-- you know, because it was swollen, and lay in the snow to cool off.
And I, one time, had a punishment.
I got 10.
Now-- why I was punished?
We-- I was chosen to go to Laurahutte-- to work in Laurahutte.
In Laurahutte, we went outside of that camp, and we boarded a train-- a street car.
And the street car went across Katowice.
And on the other side of Katowice was Laurahutte.
And Laurahutte must have been a new development, because there were building.
And the way they were building, they had diggers-- big diggers-- and they were digging the ground and loading the ground in small little wagons, and with a little choo-choo train in the front.
And they were bringing it a few kilometers outside to be dumped.
And on the way, there where rails-- so there were, you know, directional rails what had to be set.
That train goes this way, that train that way.
Now, I was chosen to go and clean all these switches.
And so I had a broom, and I had a shovel.
And I could travel with the train-- hang on the train, and travel.
And that's what I did.
And the boss was also a valse deutche-- a Polish with German descent.
He kind of like me.
Oh, you're making a good job.
You make a good job.
So good.
And on the way, there was a place where they had a big kitchen.
And in that-- that was for the SS, and for the German descent people what were working there.
And where my train went by, that was in the back.
And there were garbage cans.
And one time, when I drove by, I saw something in the garbage cans what looked like food.
So I went off, and I made like I cleaned the rails, you know.
And then slowly, slowly, I went to the garbage cans.
And that there were salad and cabbage pieces.
And-- the middle of cabbage-- and some red beets and so.
And I filled up my pockets.
And the next train who came-- and then I cleaned till the next train.
I went on the train, I went further.
And I had to eat it somewhere.
So I went into another building, which looked to me like an old building.
Nobody worked there.
Nobody was doing nothing there.
And probably was ready to be destroyed, that part of the building, because they slowly came, you know, to dig there.
And I was sitting in that building and I was eating my-- the cabbage, and whatever I got.
It took a while.
In the meantime, they were looking for me.
And when I came out, the SS man was yelling.
It wasn't SS, I'm sorry.
At that time all the firemen-- the captain was the boss, and then all the rest were old firemen-- with guns, you know.
And they were our guarders.
So he was yelling at me, and said, well, I have to report you, you know?
I told him, I just went to make pee, you know?
And, why didn't you go then?
I said, because I came with-- with the train, you see?
And I needed to go pee.
So I went there, in the old building.
So he said, it doesn't matter.
We were looking for you around, and were yelling at me.
That's why I got my 10.
That-- the same evening, when we came home after supper, my name was called.
I had to undress.
And I had to lay down on the table.
And I got my 10.
And it wasn't very pleasant.
And when I went-- Thank you.
When I went, I went to get--
We ran out of tape.
